page: Amazon Web Services m5ad.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring an AMD EPYC 7571 processor built on the x86_64 architecture. It provides 4 vCPUs, consisting of 2 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, running at a clock speed of 2.2 GHz. The instance is equipped with 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, translating to 4.0 GB of RAM per core. A key hardware feature is the inclusion of a 150 GB local NVMe SSD instance store volume, managed via the AWS Nitro hypervisor.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the m5ad.xlarge sits on the lower end of the compute spectrum. Single-core and multi-core CPU performance tests, such as stress-ng and Geekbench, place the instance in the bottom 10% tier. Memory bandwidth tests show weak to poor performance, with read-only large block bandwidth at 26.66 GB/sec and write bandwidth at 13.97 GB/sec. However, cryptographic performance is a relative highlight, with the Passmark CPU encryption test achieving an average rating in the middle 50% due to hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI instruction sets.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m5ad.xlarge offers a budget-friendly entry point for workloads requiring local NVMe storage without the premium of high-tier compute instances. The primary tradeoff is the older AMD EPYC architecture, which results in lower processing throughput and slower memory speeds compared to newer generations. The baseline network bandwidth is also limited to 1 Gbps, and there are no complimentary public IPv4 addresses included.

Given its balanced resource allocation and local NVMe storage, the m5ad.xlarge is suited for general-purpose workloads that benefit from low-latency scratch space. Appropriate workloads include development and testing environments, basic web serving, lightweight database caching, and administrative scripting tasks that do not demand intensive CPU or high-speed network throughput.
description: Amazon Web Services m5ad.xlarge is a general-purpose instance powered by a 2.2 GHz AMD EPYC 7571 processor with 4 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of DDR4 RAM. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, it features a dedicated 150 GB local NVMe SSD instance store, providing low-latency local storage. Benchmark data indicates modest processing capabilities, with single-core and multi-core CPU performance ranking in the bottom 10%, and memory bandwidth showing weak to poor results. However, it maintains average performance in cryptographic operations due to hardware-accelerated AES and SHA instruction sets. With a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps and no complimentary public IPv4 addresses, this instance represents a cost-efficient option for workloads requiring local scratch storage. It is best suited for development environments, light web serving, and basic database operations.

tagline: A budget-friendly general-purpose instance featuring dedicated AMD EPYC processing and integrated local NVMe SSD storage for low-latency scratch space.
